risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In 2007, at the start of the program, 70 y más established two eligibility criteria: (1) beneficiaries had to be 70 years old and over and (2) had to reside in localities of 2,500 or less inhabitants. We used these eligibility criteria to design our impact evaluation study and to identify the group exposed to the program ... and to establish three control groups..." Comment: Allocation/exposure was determined by pre-specified eligibility rules (age and locality size), not by a random allocation sequence. This is explicit evidence of a non-random method, so random sequence generation is at high risk of bias.

Quote: "70 y más established two eligibility criteria: (1) beneficiaries had to be 70 years old and over and (2) had to reside in localities of 2,500 or less inhabitants. We used these eligibility criteria to design our impact evaluation study and to identify the group exposed to the program (70–74 years of age, in localities with 2,500 or less inhabitants) and to establish three control groups..." Comment: Group assignment was determined by explicit, observable eligibility rules (age and locality size), so assignment was foreseeable before enrolment/interview. There was no concealed allocation mechanism such as central randomization or SNOSE; recruiters could know in advance whether a participant would be in the intervention or control group.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "70 y más established two eligibility criteria: (1) beneficiaries had to be 70 years old and over and (2) had to reside in localities of 2,500 or less inhabitants. We used these eligibility criteria to design our impact evaluation study and to identify the group exposed to the program..." and "receiving a stable and fixed monetary income was declared by OA... to be an event which had never before been experienced" and outcomes included "Depressive symptoms (DS) were assessed using the Geriatric Depression Scale" plus empowerment based on whether "the older adult declared that he participated" in decisions. Comment: This was an eligibility-based cash transfer program, so participants and personnel administering the intervention would necessarily know who was receiving the pension versus not receiving it; blinding was not feasible. The main outcomes for this study are subjective/self-reported mental well-being and empowerment measures, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of receiving the intervention. Therefore performance bias is high.

Quote: "OA were interviewed at home by standardized personnel working for the National Institute of Public Health... data collected featured... physical and mental health" and "Depressive symptoms (DS) were assessed using the Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) 15 items version" and for empowerment outcomes "the older adult declared that he participated in important (non-economic) household decisions; the second was equivalent to one where the older adult declared that he participated in household decisions pertaining to expenses." Comment: The outcome assessor for the key outcomes was primarily the participant (older adult self-report), with interviewers recording responses. The outcomes include subjective measures—depressive symptoms and self-reported empowerment/decision-making. Because this was a non-randomized social pension evaluation with participants necessarily aware of whether they received the pension, and no blinding of outcome assessment is reported, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence measurement of these self-reported outcomes. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective self-report outcomes makes this domain high risk.

Quote: "Out of the targeted 6,000 interviews of OA, 5,465 OA (a 91% response rate) were interviewed during baseline. The follow-up survey was conducted in November-December 2008, and it was possible to locate and interview 5,270 OA, or 96% of the OA interviewed at baseline." and "Inclusion criteria for the present study sample were those subjects with information on both measurements: baseline and follow up... of the 5,270 observations existing in both baseline and follow-up (Figure 1), 802 had cognitive impairment in either baseline or follow-up, and including those who had the caregivers respond to all sections with the exception of the sections on mental health, the final sample for the mental health indicators were 4,468 OA." Comment: The paper reports overall loss from baseline to follow-up (195/5465, about 4%) and additional exclusion from the mental health analysis (802/5270) due to cognitive impairment/caregiver response limitations, but it does not provide these missing/excluded numbers separately for intervention and control groups or reasons by arm. Because per-arm analysed denominators at follow-up for the outcomes of interest cannot be determined, attrition bias on the analysed outcomes cannot be judged confidently.
